_**User**_

I'm in the process of revamping this portfolio website's design. current version is a bit too basic and animations are too basic.

Another AI designing tool was used to prepare some design samples and they are better but not quite there yet. all 3 designed added to the folder @NewDesignSamples/ 

version 1 - @NewDesignSamples/Portfolio Bold.dc.html 
version 2- @NewDesignSamples/Portfolio - Ledger.dc.html 
version 3 - @NewDesignSamples/Portfolio - Overclock.dc.html 

no one of them is good by itself. version 1 is the best stand along but I like elements of the other two versions

i like the design of "From hello to in production." section in overclock, but I want that design and its animation to be used for completed projects section

and I like the "How a project actually runs." in ledger but its a bit basic it needs to be improved and made way cooler with better animations. 

I like the hero section in version 1

now using all these advice and instructions, make a brand new design for my portfolio. the old portfolio has been moved to folder /src1 and an empty folder named src has been made for the new implementation using the design concepts given and instructions given. for now, don't reference @ElementSamples/ I will tell later how to use those. right now implement the new version of the portfolio and then I will tell further improvements

---

Reviewing the three design samples and the existing portfolio content to build the new design.

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The src folder is empty. I will build the complete new portfolio implementation from scratch.

The new structure will include global CSS, centralized content data, and dedicated hooks for scroll effects, reveals, count-ups, reduced motion, custom cursor, and magnetic interactions. Component files will cover the hero canvas, marquee, services, process, projects, metrics, about, contact, and theme switcher sections.

Bold Version 1 will serve as the base aesthetic with its dark theme, Archivo and Martian Mono fonts, and eclipse color scheme. The hero canvas will feature cursor effects, magnetic interactions, and wipe reveals. Project cards will adapt Overclock’s sticky stacking design to Bold’s dark palette. The process section will enhance Ledger’s build log with scroll-driven line fills, animated nodes, staggered text reveals, glow pulses, and horizontal progress indicators.

Project cards will stack vertically on scroll with distinct background colors, large numbering, stamps, titles, blurbs, and tags. The process timeline will highlight the active step with scale and glow, slide content in from the left, pulse the connecting line, and display a step counter.

I will keep the implementation closer to the design samples by using vanilla JavaScript and CSS hooks rather than switching to framer-motion. Framer-motion may be used sparingly for the enhanced process section. All portfolio data will be extracted from the Bold sample into a centralized content file.

I will create all files directly without running terminal commands. The email address aditya.nambidi@gmail.com was decoded from the Cloudflare-protected strings in the design samples. App.css will hold global CSS variables and base resets.

A `src/styles/` directory will hold global variables and resets alongside component-level CSS. The concrete file list is now set: `main.jsx`, `App.jsx`, `App.css`, `content.js`, and twelve dedicated hook files including `useScrollEngine` for marquee and parallax effects, plus `useBuildLog` for the enhanced process timeline.

The concrete file list now covers all 23 components, from CustomCursor through Reveal. I will also update index.html with the font links.</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

The new portfolio is implemented in `src/`. Here’s how the three design samples were combined:

### Design mashup

| Section | Source | What was built |
|---------|--------|----------------|
| **Hero** | Bold v1 | Magnetic dot canvas, custom cursor, wipe reveals, parallax, bold typography |
| **Nav, Marquee, Services, Metrics, About, Contact** | Bold v1 | Dark Eclipse theme, Archivo + Martian Mono, hover rows, count-up metrics |
| **Projects** | Overclock | Sticky stacking cards that pin and layer as you scroll — same interaction as “From hello to in production,” applied to all 6 projects |
| **Process** | Ledger (enhanced) | Scroll-driven build log with progress bar, glowing rail, pulsing active nodes, card highlight + slide-in on the active step |

### File structure

```
src/
├── App.jsx / App.css
├── main.jsx
├── data/content.js          ← all copy, projects, themes
├── hooks/                   ← scroll, reveal, cursor, build log
└── components/
    ├── Hero.jsx + HeroCanvas.jsx
    ├── Process.jsx          ← enhanced build log
    ├── Projects.jsx         ← sticky stack cards
    └── … (Nav, Services, Metrics, About, Contact, etc.)
```

The old site remains untouched in `src1/`. Three themes (Eclipse, Halftone, Ember) are available via the bottom-right switcher.

### Run it

In Command Prompt from the project folder:

```cmd
npm run dev
```

Then open the URL Vite prints (usually `http://localhost:5173`).

When you’re ready, share what you want refined next — e.g. project screenshots, tighter mobile layout, or pulling in animations from `ElementSamples/`.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The Bold sample uses a single header container with padding: 120px 34px 44px. All content sits in one flex column. This structure should fix the hero placement.

The current hero splits content into separate `section-inner` divs, which likely breaks the flex layout. The canvas may also fail to span the full viewport width.

The canvas being the first child likely prevents the top row from pushing to the top. Parallax on the wrapper may also disrupt the layout. Both content sections must sit inside a single flex container.

The canvas is absolutely positioned, so it does not affect the flex layout. The hero-top and hero-main divs are the actual flex children. The top row should push to the top via margin-bottom:auto. The real problem may be missing flex:1 or insufficient height. The section-inner wrappers could also be restricting the width.

The padding values match the Bold reference exactly. The canvas positioning logic appears correct. The data-parallax transform on the hero-top wrapper may be creating a new stacking context that disrupts the flex layout.

The reveal opacity of zero on initial load may be making the hero appear broken. The plan is to restructure the hero into a single inner wrapper with no double section-inner divs. The header will use a flex layout with min-height: 100vh, max-width: 1320px, and centered padding of 120px 34px 44px.

The hero header should use `justify-content: flex-end` to pack content to the bottom. The top row will use `margin-bottom: auto` to push itself to the top. The inner wrapper needs `flex: 1` and a minimum height calculated from the viewport minus the header padding.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Restructuring the hero section into a single container to match the Bold reference design exactly.

Applying direct padding to the hero container instead of nested wrappers. Moving parallax transforms to individual elements rather than the flex parent to preserve vertical centering.

The hero-top element is double-wrapped and carries both data-parallax and section-inner classes, which causes the messy scroll reveal. I will replace this with a single `.hero-shell` wrapper matching the Bold reference exactly. The canvas will span the full viewport while the content stays centered with a max-width of 1320px. The flex layout will collapse hero-top and hero-main into one `.hero-content` column with flex-grow and a minimum height of 100vh minus the navigation bar.

The hero markup now uses a single `<header class="hero">` containing a canvas and a `.hero-content` flex column. The hero container gets `min-height: 100vh`, `max-width: 1320px`, and centered padding. The content column uses `flex: 1` and a calculated minimum height to fill the remaining viewport. The top bar will use `justify-content: space-between`.

The hero will use a direct flex column with `justify-content: flex-end` and `margin-bottom: auto` on the top row to match the Bold reference exactly. The `.section-inner` wrapper is being removed entirely; padding will be applied directly to the `.hero` container.

Parallax transforms will move to individual tag and status elements only. This prevents layout shifts while preserving the staggered title effect.

The process section will become a four-column grid. Each column shows a number, title, and description. Staggered reveal delays will animate the cards in. A subtle hover accent will tint the top border of each column.</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The `useReveal` hook likely needs adjustment. Its `-8%` bottom rootMargin may prevent hero items from intersecting on initial load.

The hero items should intersect fine despite the negative rootMargin. The real issue is asynchronous IntersectionObserver updates, which can cause a flash of invisible content. Adding an immediate viewport check on mount would prevent this. Parallax and reveal transforms are compatible once the reveal class is applied.

The reveal animation is being interrupted because the parallax engine overwrites transforms every frame. This clashes with the reveal CSS that clears transforms on activation. The Bold sample avoids this by using inline style transitions instead of CSS classes.

Removing parallax from hero title spans would stop the transform overwrite during wipe animations. Another option is to update the scroll engine to skip unrevealed elements. The best approach is to align with the Bold sample and keep parallax transforms on those elements.

Bold likely accepts the same brief transform conflict during reveal. The simplest fix is removing parallax from the hero title spans entirely. Parallax could remain on the hero-top wrapper instead.

The Bold sample intentionally places parallax on title spans, unlike the current setup. Removing parallax from the hero title spans would fix the animation conflict, but that would diverge from the reference design.</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Fixing the hero layout to match Bold's structure, resolving parallax/reveal conflicts, and replacing the process section with Bold's minimal 4-column grid plus subtle hover animations.

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Here's what was wrong and what changed:

### Hero — layout fix
The hero was split across two `section-inner` wrappers, which broke Bold's flex layout (`margin-bottom: auto` on the top row wasn't working against a single column).

It now matches **Portfolio Bold**:
- One `<header>` with `padding: 120px 34px 44px`
- Top meta row + title block as **direct flex children**
- Top row pinned up, headline + CTAs anchored at the bottom

Also fixed **parallax fighting reveal animations** — parallax no longer runs on elements until their reveal finishes, so the wipe on "AI that / earns its / keep." plays cleanly.

### Process — simplified to Bold style
The build log (progress bar, timeline rail, glowing nodes) was removed. It was heavy and didn't match Bold.

Replaced with Bold's **4-column grid**:
- Label: `(02) — How it works`
- Title kept: **How a project actually runs.**
- Large accent numbers, uppercase titles, muted descriptions
- Subtle hover: accent line draws in on top, light background tint, number lifts slightly

### Projects — toned down
Sticky stack kept, but styling aligned with Bold:
- `var(--card)` / `var(--panel-bg)` instead of full accent backgrounds
- Simpler 2-column card layout (number + content)
- Tighter sticky offsets so cards don't pile up as aggressively

---

Refresh the dev server to see the changes:

```cmd
npm run dev
```

If anything still feels off, tell me which section and I'll tune it further.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I have a thorough understanding of all 6 element samples. Let me plan which animations to use where:

1. **scrollAnimation.tsx** - ContainerScroll: A 3D perspective scroll effect where content tilts and scales. This would work perfectly for wrapping a **featured project showcase** — showing a "dashboard/terminal" mockup that comes up as you scroll.

2. **scrollScribble.tsx** - SVG path that draws itself as you scroll. Perfect for the **process section** — draw a connecting line/path between the 4 steps as you scroll through them.

3. **shaderAnimation.tsx** - WebGL shader background with animated concentric ring patterns. 

This works great as a background for the **contact section** or **hero** — it could replace or enhance the dot canvas effect.

4. **textHoverAnimation.tsx** - SVG text with gradient reveal on hover using GSAP. I'd use this for a **large section heading** like "Got busywork AI could do?" in the contact section, or the hero title.

5. **textScroll.tsx** - Characters that animate in from spread-out positions as you scroll into view. This suits the **metrics section** or a **"The stack I build on"** section where tech names assemble themselves.

6. **morphImagesAnimation.tsx** - Still evaluating...

The morph animation cycles through scatter, line, circle, and arc phases — it's complex and needs images, so it could work as an **about section** element or a decorative piece. I might adapt it more lightly for the **stack chips** in the About section instead.

For the hero, I'm thinking of replacing the dot canvas with the **shader animation** (WebGL concentric rings) as the background for more visual impact. The project already has Three.js as a dependency, but this is GPU-intensive and might be too distracting. I'm leaning toward using it as a subtle accent in the contact section instead, or as a small overlay rather than the full background.

Now I'm mapping out where each animation fits best: the **magnetic dot canvas** stays in the hero, then I'll add **ContainerScroll** below to introduce the projects section with a 3D tilt effect. The **scrollScribble SVG** connects the process steps as you scroll, **ContainerScroll** wraps a terminal mockup in the projects showcase, **CharacterV1** animates the metric numbers in from scattered positions, **CharacterV2/V3** handles the stack chips in the About section, and **TextHoverEffect** applies to the contact headline.

Since this is a plain React + Vite setup without Next.js or Tailwind, I need to install **gsap** and **@gsap/react** alongside the already-available **framer-motion** and **three.js**, plus add **lenis** for smooth scrolling. I'll translate any Tailwind styles to plain CSS and skip the `"use client"` directives. For the hero, I'm planning to enhance the title with character-level animations using framer-motion for a more polished reveal effect. me narrow down to the animations that'll have the most visual impact without overcomplicating things. The ContainerScroll effect on the projects section, the SVG scribble drawing through the process steps, the text hover reveal on the contact heading, and the character fan-in for the hero title are all worth implementing. I'll skip the morphing images animation since it's less essential for the overall polish.

Actually, I should keep the hero section as-is since the wipe reveals are already working well. Instead, I could use the character fan-in concept for a bold statement between sections, or maybe apply it to the metrics area. Let me map out what I'm actually working with: the hero with dot canvas, the marquee, services section, process, projects, metrics, and about. That gives me clear spots to layer in these animations strategically.

For the process section, I need to adapt the SVG scribble to work with the 4-column grid layout—maybe a horizontal line connecting them as you scroll. The projects section could use a tilted card reveal for the header. In contact, I'll make the main heading interactive with a hover-reveal gradient effect, and add a subtle shader animation as a decorative accent. The character spread animation could work well for the hero subtitle or another bold moment.

Now I need to actually start building this. First I'll set up gsap, then write out the component adaptations.

I should strip out TypeScript types since this is plain JSX, convert Tailwind classes to inline styles or CSS, match the existing dark background with that CDFB5B accent color, and remove any Next.js-specific directives.

For the process section, I'm drawing a horizontal wavy SVG path that connects through each step and animates its stroke as you scroll. The contact heading gets a pure JavaScript text hover effect—actually, I realize the TextHoverEffect relies on GSAP, so I need to install that dependency first. Let me think about whether I can simplify it without GSAP or if I should just add the library. The core effect is an SVG text with a gradient reveal on hover...

Writing component code... I'll put the shader animation in the contact section background as a subtle, muted effect. For the colors, I can either modify the fragment shader to emphasize the accent color ratios or use CSS blend modes to tint it toward the lime-green. Let me consider the simplest approach first.

Now I'm mapping out the key animations I want to add: animated hero text with staggered words, an SVG line connecting the process steps, a 3D tilt showcase for featured projects, a text hover effect on the contact heading, and fan-in scroll animations for the about section stack. The shader will sit as a decorative element in the about or metrics area.

I've got framer-motion and three already installed, so I can start building these out without additional dependencies. a featured terminal/dashboard mockup with 3D perspective tilt in the projects section. For the about section, I'll scatter the stack chips across the viewport and animate them into place as the section scrolls in. The contact heading gets a cursor-following gradient reveal effect using SVG and React state. I could also add a Three.js shader as a decorative background panel in the metrics section, but I want to be strategic—keep what's already working like the hero canvas and wipe reveals, layer in framer-motion animations where they add value, and maintain the dark design consistency throughout.

Now I'm thinking through which animations actually make sense together. Too many different styles could feel scattered, so I should be selective about what to implement.

The strongest choices are the SVG path scribble for the process section, the 3D tilt effect for projects, a text hover reveal for the contact heading, a shader background for the metrics area, and a scatter-assemble animation for the stack chips. I'll also enhance the hero with a staggered title animation.

Actually, given how much work this is, I should narrow it down to the 4-5 most impactful animations and execute them really well rather than spreading myself thin. Starting with the hero title using framer-motion stagger to replace the current CSS reveal approach. . Contact - TextHoverEffect + shader background, and About - scatter-assemble for the stack chips.

Now I'm writing out the implementation. For the ContainerScroll in Projects, I'll position it before the sticky stack and create a stylized terminal or dashboard that rotates from 20 degrees down to flat as you scroll. The SVG scribble in Process connects the four column headers with an organic path that animates based on scroll position using pathLength. The TextHoverEffect heading in Contact will track the cursor and reveal itself through a radial gradient. For the scatter-assemble animation in About, I'm handling the chip layout and assembly effect with framer-motion.

Writing component code...

For the hero animation, I'm thinking through how to layer framer-motion stagger on top of the existing reveal system without conflicts—I should use motion.div with variants for the title specifically and remove the data-reveal attribute to avoid duplication.

Writing the Hero component... Building the hero title with animated line breaks... Writing component markup... Writing the SVG path... the Three.js shader to sit behind the contact heading with reduced opacity so the text stays readable, and I'll shift the color palette toward the accent green. For the chip scatter animation, I'm using Framer Motion's scroll tracking with each chip starting at random offsets and assembling into place as the about section enters view—`whileInView` with variants feels like the cleanest approach. Now I need to map out the actual files to create and modify, starting with the new components and utilities I'll need.

Writing component structure... Writing the component code...

Now I'm thinking about the bigger picture for making this site visually impressive. The hero title needs a staggered line-by-line reveal effect where text slides up into view, which would be cleaner with Framer Motion's stagger capabilities than just CSS alone.

Then there's the process section with an SVG that draws as you scroll, the featured projects card with 3D perspective tilt, a contact heading with a cursor-gradient text effect, the about section with chips that scatter and reassemble, metrics that animate in with character spread, and a shader animation that could work as a subtle background layer in the hero or contact area. The shader might actually replace or enhance the existing HeroCanvas component.

I'm thinking the magnetic dots in HeroCanvas are already visually strong, so the shader would compete rather than complement. I'll place it in the contact section corner instead. Now I need to write out all the code, prioritizing the most impactful animations first. I should check my dependencies—framer-motion and three are already installed, and while GSAP would be useful for the text hover effect, I can implement the stroke dash animation with CSS and handle the cursor gradient updates through React state instead, so I don't actually need to install anything new.

Let me map out my focused implementation plan: the hero title animates each line clipping from the bottom and sliding up with staggered timing, followed by the description and CTAs fading in; the process section gets an organic SVG path that draws itself as the section scrolls into view; and the projects section opens with a 3D tilt reveal card before transitioning into the sticky stack.

For the metrics, I'm animating each number to spread out and then converge back into place as they come into view. The contact heading uses an SVG with a cursor-following gradient mask that creates an interactive stroke effect. The about section chips scatter randomly and then assemble as they scroll into view, while the contact shader adds a subtle Three.js backdrop with accent color tinting.

Now I'm starting to build out the Hero component...

I need to be careful that the framer-motion animations on the hero elements don't conflict with the reveal hook, so I'm keeping them separate without adding the data-reveal attribute. For the Process section, I'm designing an SVG path that decoratively spans across the 4-column grid with a flowing scribble effect.

Writing component code... Refining the SVG path...

Now I'm building the TextHoverEffect component for the contact section using pure React instead of GSAP. I'll layer three text elements in an SVG—a base stroke, an animated stroke that draws on, and a cursor-tracked gradient mask using the accent color. The stroke animation will use CSS keyframes to create the drawing effect.

For the ContactShader, I'm adapting the fragment shader to use lime-green tones with reduced opacity as an overlay on the panel background, keeping it contained rather than full-screen.

Now I'll write out all the component files in order: updating Hero and Process sections with the bezier path, creating the FeaturedProject component, adding the TextHoverHeading to Contact, and implementing the scatter chips animation in About using Framer Motion's viewport-triggered variants.

For the About chips, I need to be careful with hydration since this is client-side Vite—I'll use `useMemo` with seeded random values to ensure consistent positioning across renders, and for the ProcessPath I'll leverage `useRef`, `useScroll`, and `useTransform` to animate the SVG path as the user scrolls.

Writing all the component files now... Writing component markup...</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Everything looks great. Let me give you a summary of everything implemented, along with the run command to preview it:

---

Here's what was implemented across all 6 element samples:

**1. Hero — per-line clip reveal** (`scrollAnimation` + `morphImages` stagger concept)
Each title line (`"AI that"`, `"earns its"`, `"keep."`) now slides up from beneath a clipped overflow container using framer-motion, with a staggered 0.13s delay per line. The meta tags and bottom row fade up separately. This gives the classic high-end editorial reveal effect.

**2. Process — SVG scribble path** (`scrollScribble`)
An organic wavy SVG path runs across the full section width, drawing itself from left to right as you scroll into the Process section. Built with framer-motion's `useScroll` + `useTransform` driving `pathLength` from 0→1, with a subtle glow filter on the accent-colored stroke.

**3. Projects — 3D perspective tilt terminal card** (`scrollAnimation` ContainerScroll)
A new `FeaturedWork` component sits above the project stack, showing a realistic terminal window (styled like a macOS dark terminal) running an AI email-parsing job. The card is wrapped in `perspective: 1200px` and uses `rotateX` from 22°→0° + scale + opacity as you scroll to it — the same core mechanics as ContainerScroll.

**4. About — scatter-assemble stack chips** (`textScroll` CharacterV1 + `morphImages` scatter concept)
Each stack chip starts at a random scattered position (`x`, `y`, `rotate`, `scale`) derived from a seeded deterministic function. On scroll into view, they all assemble into place with a stagger of 0.04s per chip. Hover uses framer-motion `whileHover` for the accent background snap.

**5. Contact — cursor-following SVG gradient heading + shader background** (`textHoverAnimation` + `shaderAnimation`)
The "Got busywork / AI could do?" heading is now a pure SVG with 3 layers: a dim base outline, an animated stroke-draw that plays on mount (CSS `stroke-dashoffset` animation), and a cursor-following radial gradient mask that reveals an accent linear gradient wherever the mouse hovers. Behind the entire contact section, a Three.js shader renders concentric lime-green ring patterns at 8% opacity — barely visible but gives the section subtle visual depth.

To preview, run:
```
cd d:\Aditya\Programming\portfolio && npm run dev
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I understand the Ledger timeline style:

1. **Vertical timeline** with a rail/fill line on the left side
2. **Nodes** (circles) on the rail that get filled with accent color as you scroll past them
3. **Each entry** has: stamp (DAY 00, WEEK 01, etc.), title, description
4. **Content** is offset to the right of the rail (padding-left: 56px)
5. The fill line goes from top, growing as you scroll through the section
6. Nodes pulse/activate when the fill line reaches them

The user wants this Ledger-style vertical timeline BUT:


- With cooler, more polished animations
- Better overall visual design
- Without the scribble animation

So I'm replacing the current grid layout with a vertical timeline that uses framer-motion for smooth scroll-driven animations—the rail fill will track scroll progress, nodes will pulse and change color as they activate, and entries will slide in from the left as they come into view.

I'm also planning some visual enhancements: stamp badges in monospace, richer step content with titles and descriptions, a subtle glow effect on the active node, and a thoughtful layout with the stamps on the left, the rail in the center, and the content on the right. I'm considering whether to stack everything vertically or alternate the content left and right for more visual interest.

For the dark portfolio specifically, I need to adapt the color scheme—using a subtle rail color like a semi-transparent white or the panel line variable, filling it with the accent color as you scroll, and styling the nodes to glow when active. The text will use the ink color variable, and I'm thinking about making the step titles large and animated as they come into view.

With Framer Motion, I'll hook into the scroll position of the entire section to drive the fill animation, then measure each node's position relative to the section start so they activate at the right moment. Each step will slide in smoothly as it enters the viewport.

Building the Process component with this timeline approach... Writing the Process component... Writing component animations...

Actually, I'm realizing that `useTransform` needs to live at the component level, not inside a map, so wrapping each timeline entry in its own component is the right call. The tricky part is handling the `isActive` state to conditionally style the node—I think I'll move away from trying to drive everything through Framer Motion and use a simpler CSS-based approach instead.

The cleanest solution is to use `useTransform` to create a `backgroundColor` MotionValue for each node that interpolates between transparent and the accent color based on where that specific step's activation threshold falls within the scroll progress. This way each `TimelineEntry` component can manage its own animation independently, which means I need to make sure each step gets its own component instance rather than trying to handle everything in a map callback.

Now I need to add the `stamp` field back into the process steps data...

Updating content structure... Building out the timeline structure...

For animations, I'm planning to use framer-motion to control the rail fill effect as each step progresses.  The nodes will pop in with a spring bounce and glow effect, while the content slides in from the left as it enters the viewport. I want to keep the visual hierarchy clean with the large ghost numbers on the left and let the scroll-driven animations do the heavy lifting.

Now I'm setting up the CSS for the overall container and layout.

Styling the rail and fill... Still writing styles... Still writing styles... So the node center aligns with the rail center. Now I'm thinking through how to handle node activation—I could track an active index in the parent component and subscribe to scroll events to determine which nodes have been passed, checking if each node's index is less than or equal to the active one. But that requires imperative scroll listeners and DOM measurements, which feels clunky.

A cleaner approach using framer-motion: I'll grab the scroll progress in the parent with `useScroll`, pass it down to each `TimelineEntry`, and have each entry calculate its own activation threshold based on its position in the timeline. Then I'll use `useTransform` to convert that into a smooth node color transition with a spring animation.

Writing the component structure... Writing component markup...

Actually, I'm realizing that `useTransform` with CSS variables won't work for color interpolation—I need to pass actual color values instead. Framer Motion can handle color transitions, but CSS custom properties don't animate the same way. Let me reconsider the approach and look at what the current theme setup provides.

The portfolio's using a dark theme, so I could hardcode the inactive node color as a dark gray, but the active state needs to match the accent color which changes depending on the theme. Rather than hardcoding colors, I'll use `useMotionValueEvent` to listen to scroll progress and update a React state for each node—this way I can apply theme-aware colors through CSS classes or inline styles that respect the current theme.

Writing the component implementation... Writing component structure... Still writing styles... I'm reconsidering the layout approach—a grid system might be cleaner than absolute positioning, but the padding-left method with careful math for node centering on the rail is actually more straightforward. Let me work out the exact positioning: with the container's padding-left at 100px and the rail at 40px absolute, the node needs to sit centered on that rail, which means calculating its left offset relative to each entry's content area. Settling on the absolute positioning approach instead—the rail sits at a fixed left position with the container padded to accommodate it, and each node positions itself relative to that offset. Setting up the grid layout... Setting up the layout structure... I'm scrapping the absolute positioning approach and switching to a simpler flex-based layout instead. I'm building a timeline component where each row contains the metadata on the left, a centered node with a connecting line, and the content on the right.

Still writing styles... Still writing styles...

The fill animation needs to work differently than a single bar approach—I can't easily animate across multiple separate elements. I'm going with per-segment animations instead, where each timeline line activates individually as the scroll reaches its corresponding node, creating that cascading effect. For the nodes themselves, I'll use motion value events to trigger activation based on scroll position.

Now I'm settling on the final component structure and layout. The timeline will have a left column for the ghost numerals, a center column for the node and connecting line, and a right column for the content. I need to clean up where the stamp goes—it should only live in the left meta area, not duplicated in the content.

I'm also adding a nice touch where the title text transitions from muted to full color as each step becomes active during scroll, creating a spotlight effect that guides the user through the timeline.

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

All done. Here's what the new Process section does:

**Layout (Ledger-inspired, 3-column grid per row):**
- **Left column** (`pt-meta`): the `stamp` text (`DAY 00`, `WEEK 01`, etc.) at the top in small accent-colored monospace, and a large ghost numeral (`01`, `02`...) below it in a very dim color
- **Center spine** (`pt-spine`): a 14px circle node sitting on a 2px connecting line that flows to the next node
- **Right content** (`pt-content`): a → arrow indicator, the step title (large, bold), and description

**Animations:**
1. **Rail fill** — each segment's connecting line (between nodes) has a separate `pt-line-fill` whose `scaleY` animates from 0→1 via `useScroll` + `useTransform` as the section scrolls through. This creates the "line drawing down" effect.
2. **Node pop** — when a node activates, it springs outward (`scale: 1.15`) with a glowing halo (`box-shadow` with accent color) using a spring transition (`stiffness: 380, damping: 22`). The inner dot scales in separately with a tighter spring.
3. **Content slide-in** — each row's content block slides in from the left (`x: -28 → 0`) with `whileInView`, triggered when 55% of the content is visible.
4. **Arrow reveal** — a `→` accent-colored arrow fades and slides in when the step becomes active.
5. **Ghost numeral color shift** — the large background number subtly shifts from `var(--panel-line)` toward the accent hue when that step is active.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user wants the Process section to look cooler and more unique. Let me think about what would make it stand out visually while keeping the Ledger timeline structure.

Ideas to make it cooler:
1. Make the section dark/inverted background (like the metrics section) — dark bg with accent colors popping
2. Make the ghost numerals much larger and positioned as a background watermark behind each step
3. Add a horizontal layout option - maybe the timeline runs left-to-right on desktop instead of vertical
4. Add more dramatic typography - the step titles could be very large
5. Make the active state more dramatic - maybe the active row expands or highlights differently
6. Add a "terminal/code" aesthetic to the stamps


7. Try a zigzag two-column layout where content alternates sides

The most striking approach would be a full-width section with a dark inverted background and massive step numbers as watermarks. I could make the timeline rail thicker with a glowing effect, add horizontal rules that draw in for each step, and use huge typography for the titles. Each step could be a full-width row with the big number on the left, an oversized title, and the description on the right.

I'm also considering adding a bottom border that animates in when a step becomes active. Another direction: a two-column alternating layout on dark background where the left half shows the big step number and stamp, the right half displays the huge title and description. Each row would be separated by a thin line that draws in, and when active, the row lights up with a brightened background and accent color shift while the vertical rail glows. Or I could go even bolder with each step as a full-width row featuring a giant numeral watermark that fades in and out, keeping the vertical timeline structure intact.

Now I'm leaning toward a dark inverted design with massive step titles around 5-7vw, laid out horizontally with the stamp on the left edge, the big title center-left, and the description on the right, with the node sitting on the timeline rail. When a step activates, the title animates to bright white, an accent underline sweeps in, and the node gets a radial glow effect. I want it to feel like a premium log entry with bold, tight typography.

I'm also adding giant muted numerals (100px+) as decorative watermarks behind each row, and a horizontal line at the top of each entry that draws in on activation. There's a progress bar at the top of the section showing overall completion. Let me build this out.</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Here's what the redesign does to make it genuinely cool:

**Dark inverted section** — The entire Process section uses `var(--panel-bg)` (the dark background), making it pop as a distinct visual block between the light sections around it. Text and accents read crisp against it.

**Sticky header band** — As you scroll through the steps, the header (`How a project actually runs.`) sticks to the top of the viewport with a blur-backed frosted bar, so you always know what section you're in. A **progress bar** sits next to the title and fills left→right as you scroll through all 4 steps — a clean status indicator.

**Full-width border draw** — Each row has a 1px top border that animates `scaleX: 0 → 1` (left to right) as the timeline rail fills to that point. The line itself is accent-tinted.

**Giant watermark numeral** — A 14vw `01` / `02` / `03` / `04` sits behind each row in very low opacity accent color, and it **clips in from the left** (CSS `clip-path: inset(0 100% 0 0) → inset(0 0% 0 0)`) when the step activates. Barely visible but adds depth.

**Pulsing ping ring** — When a node activates, an `AnimatePresence` ring animates `scale: 1 → 2.8, opacity: 0.6 → 0` on repeat, like a sonar ping, giving it a live/active feeling.

**Title color shift** — Inactive titles are nearly invisible at `rgba(255,255,255,0.22)`. When activated they snap to full brightness with a spring, and an **accent underline sweeps across** beneath them (max 180px, glowing).

**3-column grid** — stamp+node on the left, big title center, description right — making full use of screen width like the Ledger original but much bolder.

---

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Replacing per-row segmented rail lines with a single continuous vertical line across the entire timeline.

The single line will fill from top to bottom using scroll progress. Node activation thresholds will shift earlier so the final step reveals before it leaves the viewport.

Scroll offset will tighten to `['start 90%', 'end 70%']` so the fill line completes while items remain visible. Activation thresholds will shift to `(index + 0.25) / TOTAL`, or a scaled formula like `index / (TOTAL - 1) * 0.85 + 0.05`, so the last step reveals well before it exits the viewport.

The current scroll offset `end 30%` ends too early. Progress only reaches 1 once the log bottom is 30% into the viewport, so the last step stays hidden until it scrolls out. Using `['start end', 'end start']` would let progress reach 1 while the log is still fully visible.

Scroll offsets are being refined to `['start 0.9', 'end 0.6']` so progress completes while the log remains mostly visible. The activation threshold formula is now `(i + 0.2) / (TOTAL - 0.3)`, which places the first step at roughly 5%, the second at 32%, and the third at 59% scroll progress.

The scroll offset is now `['start 88%', 'end 65%']`. The activation threshold simplifies to `(index + 0.15) / TOTAL`, placing the last step at roughly 79% scroll progress. A `TimelineRail` component will render the continuous spine as an absolutely positioned layer with a ghost track and a vertically scaling fill div.

Calculating the spine rail's exact horizontal position within the three-column grid. The rail must align with the center of each node in the left column, accounting for the 160px left column width and 34px horizontal padding.

The rail will be placed at 42px from the container edge. It will stretch vertically from the first row's node center to the last row's node center using absolute positioning.

Fixed vertical positioning is impractical without knowing row heights. The spine rail will instead sit absolutely inside a wrapper that contains all rows, stretching from top to bottom of the steps container. This avoids manual alignment calculations.

A CSS variable will pin the spine to the left column so it aligns exactly with the nodes. The spine will sit absolutely inside a relative grid container, stretching from top to bottom of the steps.

The spine rail is now positioned at 41px from the container edge. Its vertical span will be calculated from the first row’s node center downward, since each row stacks a stamp above the node with a 16px gap.

The spine will be placed as an absolutely positioned column inside a relative wrapper. Nodes will overlay the spine so the line stretches continuously from the first node center to the last. This removes the need for manual vertical alignment calculations.

A dedicated 2px spine column will be added to the grid template. JavaScript refs on the first and last nodes will pin the spine top and bottom precisely. This avoids manual height calculations.

The spine will be placed at 42px from the container edge. Its vertical span will be pinned to the first and last node positions using a ref or ResizeObserver. The segmented rails will be removed from each row and replaced by a single absolute TimelineSpine component.

The cleanest structural fix is a dedicated spine column inside the timeline layout. This avoids measuring node positions with refs and keeps the spine aligned with the nodes natively.

The spine will live in a 48px-wide grid column next to the steps. The track will be centered at 48px from the left edge, spanning from the first node center to the last. The fill will scale vertically via `scaleY`.

A separate spine column would misalign with the nodes unless the rows share the same grid. The spine will instead be absolutely positioned, with its top and height measured dynamically from refs on the first and last nodes.

The spine rail is now measured dynamically with `getBoundingClientRect` on the first and last nodes. Its top position, height, and left offset are calculated relative to the steps container. A resize listener keeps these metrics accurate.</details></think>

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Replacing segmented per-row rails with one continuous spine and tightening scroll thresholds so steps activate earlier.

Two fixes:

**1. Single continuous line**
- Removed the per-row rail segments that caused gaps between nodes.
- One spine now runs from the center of the first node to the center of the last, measured with `ResizeObserver`.
- Fill is driven by one `scaleY` on that spine, so it grows smoothly top to bottom with no breaks.

**2. Faster, earlier reveals**
- Scroll range changed from `start 80% / end 30%` to `start 92% / end 72%`, so progress finishes while the section is still on screen.
- Step activation thresholds lowered from `(index + 0.55) / TOTAL` to `(index + 0.12) / (TOTAL - 0.05)` — the last step (“Handoff & support”) now activates around ~79% progress instead of ~89%.
- Animation durations shortened slightly (0.45–0.5s instead of 0.6s).

Refresh and scroll through the section — you should see one unbroken line filling down, and all four steps lighting up before they scroll out of view.

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Here's exactly what was implemented and why it's fully reversible:

**How the animation works (faithful to `CharacterV1`):**
- The banner text `"I build AI that"` and `"earns its keep."` is split character by character
- Each character gets an `x` offset of `distanceFromCenter × 52` pixels — so the characters furthest from center (the edges) fly out the most, while the center character stays almost still
- At `scrollYProgress = 0` (banner just entering the viewport) characters are spread far apart, near-invisible at the extremes
- At `scrollYProgress = 0.6` they've fully assembled into position
- `rotateX` adds a subtle 3D tilt on each character as it flies in, matching the sample exactly

**Why it's reversible:**
- Uses `useScroll({ target: bannerRef, offset: ['start 90%', 'center 30%'] })` — this binds `scrollYProgress` directly to scroll position, not a one-shot trigger
- `useTransform` is a live derived value — as scrollYProgress decreases (scrolling up), `x` and `rotateX` values increase again, spreading the characters back out
- No `once: true`, no `whileInView`, no `animate` state — pure reactive transforms
- Scroll up past the banner and they scatter; scroll back down and they reassemble

**Section label** moved into the banner so it's associated with the animation intro, and the old `(04) — Who you'd work with` label removed from `about-left` to avoid duplication.

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Here's what changed and how the whole-section approach works:

**Single scroll binding for the entire section**
One `useScroll` ref wraps the whole `<section>`. Offset `['start 98%', 'center 15%']` means:
- Progress = 0 when the section's top edge is just entering the bottom of the viewport
- Progress = 1 when the section's center reaches 15% from the top

This means the animation plays while the section is prominently on screen — not at the edge.

**What animates and when:**

| Element | Mechanic | Progress range |
|---|---|---|
| Section label | Fade + slide up | 0 → 0.25 |
| Headline chars `"I build AI that / earns its keep."` | Per-character x-spread + rotateX (CharacterV1) | 0 → 0.55 |
| Left column (bio/accolades) | Whole block slides in from −80px | 0.2 → 0.7 |
| Right column wrapper | Slides in from +80px | 0.25 → 0.75 |
| Lead text words | Per-word x-spread + y-drop (word-level CharacterV1) | 0.1 → 0.65 |
| Body paragraph | Fade + slide up | 0.35 → 0.7 |
| Capability cards | Each card spreads outward from center-x | 0.35 → 0.78 |
| Stack header | Fade + x-slide | 0.55 → 0.78 |
| Stack chips | Scatter-assemble (existing, once) | whileInView |

**All reversible** — every `useTransform` except the stack chips tracks scroll position live, so scrolling back up reverses each layer.

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Here's what changed and how the morph mechanic is applied:

**The core adaptation from `morphImagesAnimation`**

The morph sample's key idea is spring physics driving elements between a *scattered* state and an *ordered* state. I took that exact mechanic — `stiffness: 40, damping: 15` springs (copied verbatim from the `FlipCard` transition) — and applied it to each service card's 4 feature pills.

**What happens on each card:**
- **Idle**: the 4 feature pills are scattered to seeded-random `x/y/rotate/scale/opacity` offsets. Each card's scatter is unique (different seeds per `cardIndex * 17 + pillIndex * 5`). Pills are dimmed to 28% opacity so you can sense them without them being distracting.
- **Hover**: pills spring-assemble into a clean 2×2 grid inside the card. The same `stiffness: 40, damping: 15` config means they overshoot slightly and settle with a satisfying organic feel — identical to the morph sample's phase transitions.
- **Unhover**: they scatter back out.

**Other improvements:**
- No CSS class-swapping (the old approach caused the vanishing bug) — everything is pure framer-motion `animate` props
- A glowing accent bar sweeps in from the left along the bottom edge of the card on hover
- Cards lift `y: -4` with a spring on hover
- A small hint text below the grid tells users to hover
- Cleaner card layout: vertical flex, title + desc fill space, pill zone is separated by a border at the bottom third

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user wants a scroll-driven animation using the morph concepts from `morphImagesAnimation.tsx`. Let me think about this carefully.

The morphImagesAnimation has these phases:
1. **Scatter** - images scattered randomly across the screen
2. **Line** - images form a horizontal line
3. **Circle** - images form a circle
4. **Bottom-strip (Arc)** - images form an arc at the bottom, with mouse parallax

The user wants this applied to the Services section as a **scroll animation** (not hover). The section can be made longer.

Key idea: Instead of images, I'll use the service cards (or elements within them). As the user scrolls:


- Phase 1 (entering): The service cards scatter across the section space
- Phase 2 (mid-scroll): They snap into a line or grid formation
- Phase 3 (fully scrolled in): They settle into their final assembled grid layout

I could also approach this as a tall sticky section where the service keywords or icons scatter across the full space initially, then spring into their respective card positions as the user scrolls down.

For a cleaner implementation, I'm thinking of using `position: sticky` with a tall scroll height, where `scrollYProgress` drives three distinct phases: cards start scattered with large offsets, then spring together as titles assemble, and finally settle into their grid positions. Each card could begin with randomized positioning that gradually converges toward the final layout. Building the Services component... Writing component animation... Now I'm applying spring smoothing to the scroll progress so the cards animate into their grid positions with that same fluid spring behavior, then I need to structure the layout with a tall outer container for scroll space and a sticky inner section that clips the card animations as they converge from their scattered starting positions into the grid.

Building the component... Setting up the scroll-driven animations for each card with staggered timing, using transform hooks to map the scroll progress to position, rotation, scale, and opacity values that animate from the scattered positions back to their final resting state. Writing component structure...

The sticky container will use a grid layout where the cards are positioned at their base grid locations, then the Framer Motion transforms scatter them from there with the scroll-driven animations. I need to set the outer scroll container to a tall height to give the scroll animation room to play out.

For the sticky section itself, I'm thinking about the overflow behavior—if I use `overflow: hidden`, the cards will appear to fly in from the edges as they animate into their grid positions, which creates a nice entrance effect. The header sits fixed at the top while the card grid animates beneath it.

For the card content, I want each one to display a number with a flag badge, a prominent title, description text, and a bulleted list of points. The scroll trigger needs to map the section's entrance and exit to the animation timeline—when the section enters the viewport, the cards start scattering from their grid positions, fully assemble around the middle of the scroll range, then hold steady as the section continues scrolling out. I'm also planning to add a fade-in for the section label before the cards begin their animation sequence.

Building out the Services component now... Writing the component code... Writing component markup... Writing component markup... Writing CSS styles... Still writing styles...

Actually, I'm realizing there's a layout issue here. If the container has `position: sticky` with `height: 100vh` and `overflow: hidden`, anything positioned outside those bounds will get clipped, which means the cards won't be able to animate in from off-screen. I need to decide between letting them overflow visibly, repositioning the animation origin to stay within bounds, or accepting the clipping as a visual effect.

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Here's exactly how the morphImagesAnimation was adapted:

**The direct parallel:**

| Morph sample | This implementation |
|---|---|
| `useMotionValue(0)` + `useSpring(progress, {stiffness:40, damping:15})` on virtual scroll | `useSpring(scrollYProgress, {stiffness:40, damping:15})` on real page scroll |
| Images start at `scatterPositions` (random ±750x, ±500y, ±90deg) | Cards start at seeded scatter (±850x, ±400y, ±28deg) |
| Phase `"scatter" → "line" → "circle"` driven by spring value | Phase `scattered → assembled grid` driven by the same spring value |
| `lerp(scatter, target, morphValue)` | `useTransform(progress, [t0,t1], [scatter, 0])` — same concept |
| Staggered card indices | Staggered `t0 = 0.08 + index * 0.07` thresholds |

**How it works:**
- The outer div is `240vh` tall — gives the scroll space needed
- The inner container is `sticky, height: 100vh, overflow: hidden` — the stage
- As you scroll through the 240vh, the spring-smoothed `progress` value drives each card from its scatter position toward `x:0, y:0, rotate:0, scale:1` — their natural grid positions
- `overflow: hidden` on the sticky stage means scattered cards are clipped at the edges, making them appear to crash in from outside the section
- **Fully reversible** — scroll back up and they scatter again, identical to the morph sample's scroll-reversal behavior
- Cards assemble staggered (card 1 first, then 2, then 3) with the characteristic spring overshoot that makes morphImagesAnimation feel alive

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Here's the thinking behind every decision:

**Why strips instead of cards:**
A 1300px-wide strip rotated 30° looks completely unrecognisable — it becomes a diagonal slash across the screen. When 5 of these scatter at different angles and then spring-assemble into a clean vertical list, the moment of "resolution" is genuinely dramatic. Square cards don't get this effect because their scatter silhouettes are too similar to their assembled silhouettes.

**Why 5 items:**
With 5 strips staggered at `t0 = 0.06 + i * 0.07`, there's now a proper wave — the first strip snaps in, then the second, etc. — much more like the morph animation's scatter→line→circle phase progression. The 280vh outer div gives plenty of scroll room.

**Strip anatomy (4 columns):**
- `52px` — monospace step number in accent color
- `1fr` — type tag (DISCOVERY / AUTOMATE / etc.) + bold title + short description  
- `120px` — **big impact metric** right-aligned (48 h / 5 k+ / 99% etc.) in accent — this is the visual highlight per strip
- `100px` — delivery timeline badge + → arrow

**Hover interaction (after assembly):**
A 2px accent bar scales up from the bottom on the left edge of each strip, the arrow shifts right and turns accent. Clean, no hover-state bugs since it's pure CSS transitions (no framer-motion state change on hover).

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Clean. Here's exactly what changed and why each part solves the edge cases:

**Trigger model (instead of scroll-driven transforms)**
- `assembled` is a boolean state. Strips use framer-motion's `animate` prop — the spring physics run to completion regardless of scroll position. If the user stops mid-animation, the spring keeps going to its target. If they reverse mid-animation, framer-motion springs back from wherever it currently is.

**Hysteresis band (flicker prevention)**
- `THRESH_IN = 0.28` — assembles when scrolling **down**
- `THRESH_OUT = 0.15` — scatters when scrolling **back up**
- The 0.13 gap = ~50vh of scroll distance. To flicker, the user would need to rapidly scroll 50vh up and down repeatedly — physically not realistic. A `stateRef` mirrors the state to prevent stale-closure issues inside the motion event callback.

**Two different springs**
- **Assembly** (`stiffness: 40, damping: 15`) — slow, organic, like the original morph sample
- **Scatter** (`stiffness: 90, damping: 14`) — faster and slightly underdamped so strips feel *thrown* out, which makes the reverse visually distinct

**Stagger direction**
- Assembles top → bottom (strip 0 first, 4 last, `0.07s` spacing)
- Scatters bottom → top (strip 4 first, 0 last, `0.045s` spacing) — faster teardown, reverse cascade

**Mount edge case** — a `useEffect` checks `scrollYProgress.get()` immediately on mount so if the page is loaded with the section already scrolled into view, it assembles without waiting for a scroll event.

_**Agent (composer-2.5)**_

Summary of changes:

- **`height: 160vh`** — section is now 60% shorter. Still enough room for the trigger to fire cleanly but scrolling through it takes roughly half as long.
- **`THRESH_IN = 0.40` / `THRESH_OUT = 0.25`** — with the shorter height, these map to sensible points: assembles when the section is comfortably centred in the viewport, and starts scattering early enough that the user can actually see the reversal before they've scrolled past.
- **Single shared `SPRING`** — both assemble and scatter use `stiffness: 50, damping: 16` so the perceived speed is identical in both directions.
